hypotheses: People increase their intimacy with a partner who is attentive to them.
claim.statement: Models of the evolution of friendship must incorporate information exchange regarding the valuation of the relationship.

data.sample_size: Study 1a: 312 participants, Study 1b: 105 participants, Study 2a, 2b, 2c: 109 participants combined
data.notes: Experiments included scenario-based designs and partner attention manipulation.

method.description: A series of scenario and laboratory experiments were conducted to test whether attention from a partner increased intimacy.
steps: Create scenarios or lab settings manipulating partner attention.
Recruit participants and assign them to different attention conditions.
Collect responses on perceived intimacy and other relevant measures via questionnaires.

results.summary: Attention from a partner significantly increased perceived intimacy across experiments, independent of any benefits provided.

numerical_results.value: Varied by condition but consistently higher with attention

metadata.original_paper_title: It's the attention that counts: interpersonal attention fosters intimacy and social exchange
